cot_prefix: Okay, I need to figure out the answer to this question. The question is about a passage regarding the copyright of "Happy Birthday to You" and asks what the history experts' statements prove. The options are (A) to (D). Let me read through the passage again to make sure I understand it. The main points are: - Jennifer Nelson had to pay $1,500 to use "Happy Birthday to You" in her documentary, which is about the history of the song. The money went to Warner Music Group, who claim they own the copyright since 1988. - The original song was written by the Hill sisters as "Good Morning to All" in 1893. People then started using the "Happy Birthday" lyrics with the same tune. - History experts say there's no record of who wrote the "Happy Birthday" lyrics and that the public was singing it long before it was printed and owned by a company. - Nelson's lawyers argue this history shows the song belongs to the public, so Warner Music shouldn't charge fees. They want a court to make Warner return fees collected in the past four years. - Warner/Chappell has made about $2 million a year from licensing fees. The question is about what the history experts' statement proves regarding the "Happy Birthday" song. Let me look at the relevant part of the passage again: "A number of history experts say that there is no record of who actually wrote the 'Happy Birthday to You' lyrics. Historians also say there is no way to know when the general public began singing the 'Happy Birthday' song, but they believe it was being sung by the public long before it was printed and owned by a company." So the experts are saying two main things: 1. No record exists about who wrote the lyrics. 2. The public was singing it long before a company printed and owned it.
